name: South Dakota v. Dole
summary: Congress conditioned five percent of a state's federal highway funds on the state adopting a minimum drinking age of twenty-one, and South Dakota challenged the condition as exceeding Congress's spending power. The Court upheld the condition and articulated a four-part test for evaluating whether conditions attached to federal grants are a valid exercise of the Spending Clause.
holding: Congress may attach conditions to federal grants as long as the conditions satisfy four requirements: (1) the expenditure serves the general welfare; (2) the conditions are stated unambiguously so states can knowingly accept them; (3) the conditions are reasonably related to the federal interest in the particular program; and (4) the conditions do not require the states to engage in activities that would themselves be unconstitutional. A relatively mild financial inducement does not cross into unconstitutional coercion.

legal_definitions.definition: The four-part standard for valid Spending Clause conditions: (1) expenditure must serve the general welfare; (2) conditions must be stated unambiguously; (3) conditions must be reasonably related to the federal interest in the program; and (4) conditions must not require violation of independent constitutional prohibitions.
legal_definitions.context: Dole's four-part test controls whether Congress may attach conditions to federal grants to states; subsequent decisions added a fifth implicit element — the conditions must not be so coercive as to effectively compel compliance.

relevance_to_audit: Establishes the governing test for conditions attached to federal grants; critical for auditing whether federal technology, law enforcement, or surveillance grant conditions — including JAG, Byrne, and COPS grants — satisfy constitutional requirements and whether grantees have received adequate notice of obligations.

key_quotes.text: The spending power is of course not unlimited, but is instead subject to several general restrictions articulated in our cases.
key_quotes.context: Introducing the four-part framework for evaluating Spending Clause conditions and acknowledging constitutional limits on conditional federal grants.

subsequent_treatment: Governing Spending Clause framework; coercion element applied to invalidate Medicaid expansion mandate in NFIB v. Sebelius (2012)
